title: Severe acute pulmonary embolism in pregnancy
one_line_summary: Acute pulmonary embolism (PE) is a critical driver of maternal morbidity and mortality in pregnancy. This review outlines presentation, diagnosis, risk-stratification, and management, emphasizing standard criteria apply and thrombolysis for high-risk PE.
key_pearls: Do not delay initial therapeutic LMWH/UFH for suspected PE while awaiting imaging, if no contraindications.
Prioritize CTPA over V/Q in unstable patients for speed, RV/LV assessment, and ruling out other pathologies.
Restrict IV fluids (250-500 mL boluses) in RV strain to prevent worsening ventricular interdependence and obstructive shock.
Prepare for hemodynamic collapse during intubation; have noradrenaline ready before induction.
Reserve systemic alteplase strictly for high-risk PE (hemodynamic instability/shock) due to high postpartum bleeding risk (58.3%).
Activate a multidisciplinary Pulmonary Embolism Response Team (PERT) for intermediate/high-risk or high-risk PE.
population: Pregnant and postpartum patients with acute pulmonary embolism.

sections.heading: Epidemiology and Risk Factors
sections.content: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) affects approximately 1 in 1,000 pregnancies. The antenatal risk is 4- to 5-fold higher compared to non-pregnant peers, occurring at any gestational age. The postpartum period, especially the first 24 hours post-delivery, represents the highest-risk window with a relative risk as high as 20. Key predisposing factors include a prior history of thrombosis, active inflammatory disease, obesity, and hyperemesis gravidarum.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology of Obstructive Shock
sections.content: Acute pulmonary embolism (PE) causes a sudden, severe rise in pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) due to mechanical vascular occlusion and humoral-mediated vasoconstriction (driven by serotonin and thromboxane A2 release). The unconditioned right ventricle (RV) dilates under acute pressure and volume overload, causing the interventricular septum to bow leftward into the left ventricular (LV) cavity. This septal shifting impairs LV filling, leading to a severe drop in stroke volume, systemic hypotension, reduced coronary perfusion to the overloaded RV, worsening RV ischemia, and a rapid downward spiral toward cardiovascular collapse.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation & Differential Diagnosis
sections.content: Patients typically present with dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, and pre-syncope or syncope, which is a critical indicator of transient hemodynamic compromise. Diagnostic pitfalls arise because symptoms significantly overlap with normal physiological changes of pregnancy and other acute pathologies. Clinicians must avoid premature diagnostic closure. Key differentials include: - Pneumonia: presenting with dyspnea, pleuritic pain, sepsis-induced hemodynamic changes. - Pneumothorax: characterized by sudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, and potentially tension pneumothorax-induced shock. - Cardiac Disease: such as myocardial ischemia, acute heart failure, or arrhythmias causing dyspnea and syncope. - Aortic Dissection: more common in pregnancy, presenting with tearing chest pain. - Concealed Haemorrhage: e.g., ectopic pregnancy rupture or splenic artery aneurysm rupture, presenting with abdominal/chest pain, tachypnea, and hemorrhagic shock.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Investigations
sections.content: Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) is a highly valuable bedside adjunct (cardiac, thoracic, abdominal, and lower limb) to rule out differentials and assess RV strain. Definitive imaging must not be delayed or denied due to pregnancy; both CTPA and V/Q scans are supported by the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). A V/Q scan carries a slightly higher risk of childhood cancer but a lower risk of maternal breast cancer. CTPA is preferred in the acutely deteriorating patient due to its rapid out-of-hours availability, direct assessment of RV size, detailed anatomical clot burden mapping (to guide catheter-directed therapies), and identification of alternative cardiac or aortic pathologies. Serum troponin and B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) remain within normal reference ranges during healthy pregnancy and should be utilized for risk stratification.

sections.heading: Risk Stratification
sections.content: High-Risk PE (formerly 'Massive') is defined by cardiac arrest or hemodynamic instability, characterized by: - Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP) < 90 mmHg, OR - SBP drop of >= 40 mmHg for > 15 minutes (not caused by new-onset arrhythmia, hypovolemia, or sepsis), OR - Obstructive shock with end-organ hypoperfusion. This occurs in approximately 7% of pregnancy-associated PEs and carries a 15-30% mortality rate, requiring immediate reperfusion. Intermediate-Risk PE involves hemodynamically stable patients (SBP >= 90 mmHg) but with signs of RV dysfunction or myocardial injury. Intermediate/High-Risk (formerly 'Submassive') PE is defined by the presence of both RV dilatation (on CTPA or echo) and elevated cardiac biomarkers (troponin or BNP), carrying a 3-15% mortality rate and requiring intensive monitoring. Intermediate/Low-Risk PE involves the presence of either RV dilatation or elevated biomarkers, but not both. Regarding pregnancy physiology, SBP rarely falls below 95 mmHg in healthy pregnancy, making the standard SBP < 90 mmHg threshold highly specific for high-risk PE. The LV/RV ratio remains unchanged in normal pregnancy, so significant RV dilatation is always pathological.

sections.heading: Therapeutic Management
sections.content: Anticoagulation: The first dose should be administered immediately if PE is highly suspected, while awaiting imaging confirmation. Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is the mainstay for intermediate-risk PE, often requiring higher doses in pregnancy due to increased renal clearance. Unfractionated heparin (UFH) is preferred in high-risk PE requiring immediate reperfusion or when rapid reversibility is needed (e.g., imminent delivery, neuraxial anesthesia, or extreme bleeding risk). Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs) are strictly contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal risks. Reperfusion Therapies: Systemic thrombolysis is first-line for high-risk PE with severe hypotension, shock, or cardiac arrest; alteplase is preferred and does not cross the placenta. However, antenatal major bleeding risk is 17.5%, and postpartum risk is 58.3%, with a fetal death rate of approximately 12%. Percutaneous catheter-directed reperfusion (suction thrombectomy, low-dose catheter-directed thrombolysis) is indicated on a case-by-case basis if systemic thrombolysis is contraindicated, has failed, or during the high-risk peripartum window. Supportive Care in the ICU: Fluid management should be conservative (limited volumes) to prevent worsening RV overdistension. Intubation is an extreme hazard; induction and positive pressure ventilation can cause cardiovascular collapse, requiring senior clinicians and vasopressors. Noradrenaline is the preferred vasopressor; inodilators (milrinone, dobutamine, levosimendan) can improve RV contractility. Veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VA-ECMO) is a viable rescue therapy for refractory obstructive shock.

strengths_limitations: This review provides clear, actionable guidance bridging obstetric medicine and critical care guidelines, validating standard non-pregnant hemodynamic thresholds (e.g., SBP < 90 mmHg) in pregnancy. However, clinical evidence for thrombolysis and catheter-directed therapies in pregnancy is limited to retrospective cohorts, registries, and case series, which are prone to reporting bias.
